  
\# TA Grading Queue Organizer

\#\# Chosen Problem : 

    \# The problem I have chosen is the TA Grading Queue Organizer. It keeps track of submitted assignments based on the order/time they have been submitted. 

\#\# Chosen Algorithm : 

    \# The algorithm I have chosen to use for this problem is Merge Sort. The reason behind choosing merge sort is that it is a stable sorting algorithm which means it will preserve the order in which submissions are received. Quick sort on the other hand, would not, and might randomly swap submissions for no reason which would waste resources.

   \# Preconditions : The application requires that all of the submissions (Student ID, Time, File Name) are filled out before a submission can be added to the queue. The Gradio UI blocks empty submissions and displays an error if this isn’t done.

\*\*Visual Simulation:\*\* During the simulation, the user will see the data frame visually update step-by-step. The animation abstracts the recursive math and simply shows the rows physically re-ordering themselves as the algorithm merges the timestamps into their final chronological positions.

\#\# Screenshots of Test : 

Before Sorting :   
![][image1]

After Sorting ![][image2]

\#\# Problem Breakdown & Computational Thinking

    \# Decomposition : Breaking down this task mainly involves sorting the actual queue itself, which means recursively  splitting the list of submissions in half and keep doing so until each list is broken down to just having one value. Then, it becomes much easier to merge all the values back together based on the order they were submitted in. 

    \# Pattern Recognition : The pattern that is evident in this algorithm is the actual process of splitting and narrowing down the big list. The process is just creating small sublists, looking at the first two values of these sublists, determining which one is first, and moving that one into a new subarray. This is the only pattern in the algorithm.   

    \# Abstraction : Since this is just a visual demo, the abstraction portion of the algorithm would be not having to worry about the math, or having to show the actual recursion proces going into the final display. Also, since we are trying to visualize the actual sorting algorithm, there isn't a need to show how splitting the list each time works. 

    \# Algorithm Design : 

            \# Input : First, the TA/user would have to click "add submission", which would be received and noted within the program. Then, a small set of dictionaries would hold the student\_id, submitted\_at, and file\_name and append it to the list. 

        \# Processing : Then, the merge sort function is put into use, where every time a value is moved, it pauses for a second and takes what is pretty much a digital screenshot. 

        \# Output : Once the processing is done, the Gradio UI would take these screenshots and create essentially an animation that shows how the values actually shift around. 

\#\# Flowchart : 

\#\# Steps to Run

In order to run this locally, 

1. Download the code from this project’s main GitHub page: Click the green \<\> Code button at the top right of this repository and choose "Download ZIP".

2. Extract the folder: Unzip the downloaded file and open the folder so you can see the app.py file.  
     
3. Open your terminal: Open your command prompt or terminal directly inside this folder. Click on the top of the folder (where the name is) and type “cmd”

4. Install the required library: In the cmd, type : pip install \-r requirements.txt and press enter.

5. Start the app: Type : python app.py and hit enter

6. Open the app**:** The terminal will show a local web link (usually http://127.0.0.1:7860). Ctrl+Click on that link and the GUI simulation will open in your browser

\#\# Hugging Face Link

[https://huggingface.co/spaces/VarshanA007/TAGradingQueue](https://huggingface.co/spaces/VarshanA007/TAGradingQueue)

\#\# Testing : 

To make sure the algorithm works correctly under all cases, I manually tested a few different scenarios using the finished app :

-  Added three new submissions with wide ranging timestamps (16:00, 10:15, 12:30). Ideally, the app should sort out the combined queue in order and show an animation step by step. After trying it, the queue sorted perfectly, and the visualization was perfect.

- Cleared the default values and put in three submissions that are in perfect order just to make sure (08:00, 09:00, 10:00). The app worked through the correct order, and the animation actually showed that although the algorithm went through the comparisons, the GUI didn’t do any visual swapping.

- Added two submissions with the exact same time (Student A at 11:15, Student B at 11:15). Because Merge Sort is a stable algorithm, it correctly placed both 11:15 submissions in the right spot without flipping Student A and Student B. 

\#\# Author & AI Acknowledgment : 

While working on this project, I used a few points of reference to help me work on developing the program, as well as to better understand the submission formatting. 

The main AI use in this project was my use of Google Gemini to develop and integrate the Gradio framework into the algorithm, since Gradio was a tool that I was completely unfamiliar with, and wasn’t sure how to get started with. I have used comments to indicate exactly from which point onwards I have used the code generated by Gemini in my app.py file, and other than that it has been used to integrate the Gradio effects with the logic of the code I wrote myself. Besides everything Gradio, the rest of the code and written components of the project are all done by me.
